step.title: Step 15
step.actionDisplay: DOWN -> RIGHT -> UP -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 6 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: DOWN, RIGHT, UP, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 74, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental. REQUIRED: call `predict(expect_change=... )` (or `predict(expected_grid=...)`) before each `action(...)`; then read `last_prediction_result`/`prediction_stats`. A `surprise` marks where your world model is wrong -- re-probe there instead of pressing on.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Blocks didn't move after DOWN, contrary to earlier observation. Need to investigate why blocks moved before but not now. Perhaps blocks only move when connected by path.
- Goal model: Navigate the agent to connect to colored blocks in the order shown in bottom panel: Red → Green → Blue.
- Action model: UP/DOWN moves agent vertically by 6 rows. RIGHT extends the path rightward. Need to test what happens when path reaches a colored block.
- Plan: Check segmentation for path nodes and understand when blocks move.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 73 actions; a strong score needs about 61 or fewer. You are 1.2x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Looking at the segmentation, I see:
- The agent (M, id=11) is at rows 30-35, cols 11-16
- There are path segments (g, G colors) at cols 13-14, connecting different rows
- The blocks (R, b, N) are at cols 18-21, inside the dark gray area (c, id=1)
- The agent is adjacent to the dark gray area (1(c) -- 11(M))
- The blocks are also adjacent to the dark gray area (1(c) -- 2(R), 1(c) -- 5(b), 1(c) -- 8(N))
Looking at the path structure:
- There are gray path segments at cols 13-14, connecting rows 14-15, 16-19, 20-21, 22-25, 26-27, 28-29, 36-37, 38-39
- The agent (M) is at rows 30-35, and is adjacent to path segments at rows 28-29 (id=10, G) and 36-37 (id=16, G)
I notice the path segments are at cols 13-14, which is to the left of the agent (cols 11-16). The blocks are at cols 18-21, to the right of the agent.
Looking at the adjacency, the agent (id=11) is connected to:
- id=10 (G, rows 28-29) - path above
- id=16 (G, rows 36-37) - path below
- id=1 (c) - the dark gray area
- id=14 (w), id=15 (g) - small nodes at col 16
The key insight is that the blocks are INSIDE the dark gray area (id=1), not connected to the agent via a path. The path segments (g, G) are at cols 13-14, which is outside the dark gray area.
So the blocks don't move with the agent because they're not connected to the agent. They only move when the agent extends a path to them (RIGHT) and then retracts (LEFT).
But wait, earlier when I moved UP, the blocks DID move. Let me check what happened then. Looking at the earlier output:
- Before UP: Red 19-22, Blue 25-28, Green 31-34
- After UP: Red 13-16, Blue 19-22, Green 25-28
So the blocks moved UP by 6 rows. But now when I moved DOWN, the blocks didn't move. This is inconsistent.
Wait, let me re-check. After the earlier sequence (RIGHT x5, UP, LEFT x10), the blocks were at:
- Red: rows 13-16
- Blue: rows 19-22
- Green: rows 25-28
And the agent was at rows 24-29. Then I did DOWN, and the blocks stayed at the same positions. But earlier, when I did UP (from rows 30-35 to 24-29), the blocks moved.
Hmm, maybe the blocks only move when the agent moves UP, not DOWN? Or maybe there's a different mechanism.
Actually, let me look at the path segments more carefully. The path segments at cols 13-14 seem to be "track" segments that the agent moves along. When the agent moves, it might be pulling or pushing the blocks based on the path.
Wait, I think I see the issue. The path segments at cols 13-14 are NOT connected to the blocks. They're just track segments. The blocks are inside the dark gray area and are only affected when the agent extends a path to them.
But then why did the blocks move when I moved UP earlier? Let me check the history more carefully.
Actually, I think the issue is that the blocks are connected to the agent via the dark gray area (id=1). The adjacency list shows:
- 1(c) -- 2(R) - dark gray connected to Red
- 1(c) -- 5(b) - dark gray connected to Blue
- 1(c) -- 8(N) - dark gray connected to Green
- 1(c) -- 11(M) - dark gray connected to agent
So the blocks are connected to the agent via the dark gray area. When the agent moves, the dark gray area might be pulling the blocks.
But wait, the dark gray area (id=1) is a large region at rows 12-41, cols 17-46. It's not moving with the agent. So how do the blocks move?
Let me think about this differently. Maybe the blocks move when the agent moves because they're "attached" to the agent via some mechanism. But the mechanism might be directional - maybe blocks only move when the agent moves in a certain direction.
Actually, I think I need to reconsider the earlier observation. Let me check if the blocks actually moved or if I misread the output.
Looking at the earlier output:
```
=== After UP ===
  R: rows 13-16, cols 18-21
  b: rows 19-22, cols 18-21
  N: rows 25-28, cols 18-21
  Agent: rows 30-35
```
Wait, this says Agent is at rows 30-35 after UP. But UP should move the agent UP, not DOWN. Let me check the sequence again.
The sequence was: RIGHT x5, UP, LEFT x10. Before this, the agent was at rows 30-35 (after the DOWN action). So:
- Before RIGHT x5: Agent at 30-35
- After RIGHT x5: Agent still at 30-35 (RIGHT extends path, doesn't move agent)
- After UP: Agent should move to 24-29
- After LEFT x10: Agent still at 24-29
But the output says "Agent: rows 30-35" after UP. That's strange. Let me re-read the output.
Oh wait, I see the issue. The output says:
```
=== After UP ===
  R: rows 13-16, cols 18-21
  b: rows 19-22, cols 18-21
  N: rows 25-28, cols 18-21
  Agent: rows 30-35
```
But this is from the code that printed the agent position AFTER the UP action. The agent should be at 24-29 after UP. But the output says 30-35. This suggests that the UP action didn't move the agent, or there's an error in my code.
Wait, let me re-read the code. The code does:
```python
action(['UP'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
for n in seg:
    if n['color'] == 'M' and n['pixels'] == 18:
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        if min(rows) < 50:
            print(f"  Agent: rows {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}")
```
This should print the agent's position after UP. If the agent is at 30-35, then UP didn't move the agent. But that contradicts my understanding of the game.
Actually, wait. Let me check if there are multiple M-colored nodes. Looking at the current segmentation:
- id=11: M, pixels=18, rows 30-35, cols 11-16 (agent)
- id=13: M, pixels=4, rows 32-33, cols 13-14 (part of agent?)
- id=21: M, pixels=4, rows 56-61, cols 20-25 (bottom panel agent?)
So there are multiple M-colored nodes. The agent is id=11 with pixels=18. The code filters for pixels=18, so it should find the correct agent.
But the output says "Agent: rows 30-35" after UP. This suggests the agent didn't move. But that contradicts my earlier understanding.
Wait, let me re-check the sequence. The actions were:
1. DOWN (agent moved from 24-29 to 30-35)
2. RIGHT (extended path)
3. UP (should move agent from 30-35 to 24-29)
4. LEFT x5 (retracted path)
But the output after UP says Agent is at 30-35. This is strange.
Actually, I think I misread the output. Let me look again:
```
=== After DOWN ===
  R: rows 13-16, cols 18-21
  b: rows 19-22, cols 18-21
  N: rows 25-28, cols 18-21
  Agent: rows 30-35
=== After RIGHT (grab Green) ===
  R: rows 13-16, cols 18-21
  b: rows 19-22, cols 18-21
  N: rows 25-28, cols 18-21
=== After UP ===
  R: rows 13-16, cols 18-21
  b: rows 19-22, cols 18-21
  N: rows 25-28, cols 18-21
  Agent: rows 30-35
```
So after UP, the agent is still at 30-35. This means UP didn't move the agent! But that contradicts my earlier understanding that UP moves the agent up by 6 rows.
Wait, maybe UP only moves the agent when it's not blocked. Or maybe the agent can only move in certain positions.
Actually, I think the issue is that the agent is at the bottom of the track and can't move UP because the path is blocked. Or maybe the agent can only move DOWN, not UP.
Let me check the current state. The agent is at rows 30-35. The path segments are at:
- rows 28-29 (above agent)
- rows 36-37 (below agent)
So there are path segments both above and below the agent. The agent should be able to move UP or DOWN.
But the UP action didn't move the agent. Let me try DOWN to see if that works.
Actually, I think the issue might be that the agent is "grabbing" something and can't move. When I extended RIGHT, the path connected to Green. Maybe the agent can't move while holding a block.
Let me check if retracting LEFT releases the block and allows the agent to move.
Actually, looking at the output after retracting LEFT:
```
=== After retract ===
  R: rows 13-16, cols 18-21
  b: rows 19-22, cols 18-21
  N: rows 25-28, cols 18-21
```
The blocks are still at the same positions. So retracting LEFT didn't move the blocks either.
I think my earlier observation about blocks moving with the agent was wrong. Let me reconsider the game mechanics.
Actually, wait. Let me go back to the earlier sequence where I thought the blocks moved. The sequence was:
1. RIGHT x5 (extended path to grab Green)
2. UP (moved agent up)
3. LEFT x10 (retracted path)
And the output showed:
- Before: Red 19-22, Blue 25-28, Green 31-34
- After UP: Red 13-16, Blue 19-22, Green 25-28
But now I'm questioning whether this is correct. Let me check the history more carefully.
Actually, I think the issue is that I misread the earlier output. The blocks might not have moved at all. Let me re-examine.
Looking at the earlier output from the RIGHT x5, UP, LEFT x10 sequence:
```
=== After extending RIGHT ===
  color=R rows 19-22, cols 18-21
  color=b rows 25-28, cols 18-21
  color=N rows 31-34, cols 42-45
=== After UP ===
  Agent: rows 24-29, cols 11-16
  Agent: rows 56-61, cols 20-25
  color=R rows 13-16, cols 18-21
  color=b rows 19-22, cols 18-21
  color=N rows 25-28, cols 42-45
```
So after UP:
- Red moved from 19-22 to 13-16 (moved up 6 rows)
- Blue moved from 25-28 to 19-22 (moved up 6 rows)
- Green stayed at 25-28 (but cols changed from 42-45 to 25-28? No, wait, Green is at rows 25-28, cols 42-45)
Wait, Green is at rows 25-28, cols 42-45 after UP. So Green didn't move vertically, but Red and Blue did. This confirms that when the agent moves UP, ungrabbed blocks move with the agent, but grabbed blocks stay in place.
But now, when I tried to do the same thing (DOWN, RIGHT, UP), the blocks didn't move. Let me check what's different.
Before the earlier sequence:
- Agent was at rows 30-35
- Red at 19-22, Blue at 25-28, Green at 31-34
- I extended RIGHT to grab Green (Green moved to cols 42-45)
- Then I moved UP, and Red/Blue moved up but Green stayed
Now:
- Agent is at rows 30-35
- Red at 13-16, Blue at 19-22, Green at 25-28
- I extended RIGHT to grab Green (but Green didn't move in cols?)
- Then I moved UP, and nothing moved
The difference is that in the earlier sequence, Green moved from cols 18-21 to 42-45 when I extended RIGHT. But in the current sequence, Green stayed at cols 18-21. This suggests that the RIGHT action didn't actually grab Green.
Let me check the current state. Green is at rows 25-28, cols 18-21. The agent is at rows 30-35, cols 11-16. When I extend RIGHT, the path should connect to Green. But maybe the path doesn't connect because Green is not in the agent's row range.
The agent is at rows 30-35, and Green is at rows 25-28. They don't overlap vertically. So the path can't connect to Green.
In the earlier sequence, the agent was at rows 30-35 and Green was at rows 31-34. They overlapped vertically (rows 31-34 are within 30-35). So the path could connect to Green.
So the issue is that I need to align the agent with the block vertically before extending RIGHT. Let me try again.
Current state:
- Agent: rows 30-35
- Red: rows 13-16
- Blue: rows 19-22
- Green: rows 25-28
To grab Green, I need the agent to be at rows 25-30 (overlapping with Green's 25-28). Currently the agent is at 30-35, which overlaps with Green's 25-28 at row 30. But maybe the overlap needs to be more significant.
Actually, looking at the earlier successful grab:
- Agent: rows 30-35
- Green: rows 31-34
- Overlap: rows 31-34 (4 rows)
Current:
- Agent: rows 30-35
- Green: rows 25-28
- Overlap: row 30 (1 row)
So the overlap is only 1 row, which might not be enough to grab Green. I need to move the agent UP to align better with Green.
But wait, earlier I tried UP and it didn't move the agent. Let me check why.
Actually, looking at the output again:
```
=== After UP ===
  R: rows 13-16, cols 18-21
  b: rows 19-22, cols 18-21
  N: rows 25-28, cols 18-21
  Agent: rows 30-35
```
The agent is still at 30-35 after UP. But the blocks didn't move either. This suggests that UP didn't do anything.
Wait, but earlier UP did move the agent and the blocks. What's different?
Oh, I think I see the issue. In the earlier sequence, the agent was at rows 30-35 and Green was at rows 31-34, cols 42-45 (after extending RIGHT). So the agent was "holding" Green. When I moved UP, the agent moved to 24-29, and the ungrabbed blocks (Red, Blue) moved with the agent, but Green stayed.
But in the current sequence, after extending RIGHT, Green is still at cols 18-21. This means the RIGHT action didn't grab Green. So when I moved UP, the agent couldn't move because... why?
Actually, maybe the agent CAN move, but the UP action didn't work because the agent is at the bottom of the track and can't move further UP. Or maybe the track is blocked.
Let me try a different approach. Instead of trying to move the agent, let me try to understand the game mechanics better.
Looking at the bottom panel, it shows Pink, Red, Green, Blue. The current arrangement of blocks is Red, Blue, Green (top to bottom). The target order is Red, Green, Blue.
Maybe the goal is not to rearrange the blocks, but to connect them in the correct order. The bottom panel might be showing the order in which the blocks should be connected.
Let me try a different approach: connect the blocks in the order Red, Green, Blue. This might mean:
1. Connect to Red first
2. Then connect to Green
3. Then connect to Blue
But I've already connected to all three blocks. Maybe the order of connection matters, and I need to reconnect them in the correct order.
Actually, let me step back and think about this differently. The game might be about creating a path that connects the blocks in the correct order. The bottom panel shows the target order: Red, Green, Blue.
Currently, the blocks are arranged vertically as Red, Blue, Green. To match the target order, I need to arrange them as Red, Green, Blue.
The way to do this might be:
1. Grab Green (extend RIGHT when aligned with Green)
2. Move the agent to a position where Green will be between Red and Blue
3. Release Green (retract LEFT)
But I've been struggling with this because the agent doesn't seem to move when I try UP/DOWN.
Let me try a completely different approach. Maybe I need to use MOUSE to click on the blocks to rearrange them. Or maybe I need to use ACTION7 (even though it gave an error earlier).
Actually, let me try to move the agent by extending RIGHT further and then retracting LEFT. Maybe this will allow the agent to move.
Wait, I just realized something. The agent is at rows 30-35, and the path segments are at cols 13-14. The blocks are at cols 18-21. When I extend RIGHT, the path extends from the agent's right edge (col 16) towards the blocks.
But the blocks are inside the dark gray area (cols 17-46). So the path extends into the dark gray area and connects to the blocks.
Let me check if the path is currently connected to any blocks. Looking at the adjacency list:
- 1(c) -- 11(M) - dark gray connected to agent
- 1(c) -- 2(R) - dark gray connected to Red
- 1(c) -- 5(b) - dark gray connected to Blue
- 1(c) -- 8(N) - dark gray connected to Green
The agent is connected to the dark gray area, and the blocks are connected to the dark gray area. But the agent is not directly connected to the blocks.
I think the dark gray area is a "container" that holds the blocks. The agent interacts with the container, and the blocks move within the container.
Let me try a different approach. Maybe I need to move the agent to different positions and the blocks will rearrange themselves based on the agent's position.
Actually, let me try to simply move the agent UP and see what happens. Maybe the earlier UP didn't work because of some temporary state, and now it will work.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Agent at rows 30-35, blocks at Red 13-16, Blue 19-22, Green 25-28. Earlier UP moved blocks but now it doesn't - need to investigate. The agent may need to be aligned with blocks to grab them.
Plan: First try moving agent UP to see if it works now, then try to grab and reposition blocks.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check current state and try moving agent
seg = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
print("=== Current state ===")
for n in seg:
    if n['color'] in ['R', 'b', 'N'] and n['pixels'] == 16:
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        if min(rows) < 50:
            print(f"  {n['color']}: rows {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, cols {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}")
for n in seg:
    if n['color'] == 'M' and n['pixels'] == 18:
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        if min(rows) < 50:
            print(f"  Agent: rows {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}")

# Try UP to move agent
predict(expect_change=True, note='Try moving agent UP')
action(['UP'])

seg = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
print("\n=== After UP ===")
for n in seg:
    if n['color'] in ['R', 'b', 'N'] and n['pixels'] == 16:
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        if min(rows) < 50:
            print(f"  {n['color']}: rows {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, cols {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}")
for n in seg:
    if n['color'] == 'M' and n['pixels'] == 18:
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        if min(rows) < 50:
            print(f"  Agent: rows {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}")

# Check if board changed
print(f"\nBoard changed: {last_action_result.get('board_changed', 'unknown')}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.content:

  N: rows 25-28, cols 18-21
  Agent: rows 30-35

=== After UP ===
  R: rows 13-16, cols 18-21
  b: rows 19-22, cols 18-21
  N: rows 25-28, cols 18-21
  Agent: rows 24-29

Board changed: True

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check current state and try moving agent
seg = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
print("=== Current state ===")
for n in seg:
    if n['color'] in ['R', 'b', 'N'] and n['pixels'] == 16:
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        if min(rows) < 50:
            print(f"  {n['color']}: rows {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, cols {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}")
for n in seg:
    if n['color'] == 'M' and n['pixels'] == 18:
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        if min(rows) < 50:
            print(f"  Agent: rows {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}")

# Try UP to move agent
predict(expect_change=True, note='Try moving agent UP')
action(['UP'])

seg = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
print("\n=== After UP ===")
for n in seg:
    if n['color'] in ['R', 'b', 'N'] and n['pixels'] == 16:
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        if min(rows) < 50:
            print(f"  {n['color']}: rows {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, cols {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}")
for n in seg:
    if n['color'] == 'M' and n['pixels'] == 18:
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        if min(rows) < 50:
            print(f"  Agent: rows {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}")

# Check if board changed
print(f"\nBoard changed: {last_action_result.get('board_changed', 'unknown')}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
